title: Daemon duplicate spawn loop - issue stays triage:ready after first agent spawned
description: Three agents spawned for the same ci-implement-role work because the issue remained triage:ready after the first spawn. The daemon's dedup TTL cache expired between spawns, allowing re-spawning of already-in-progress work. Root cause: daemon should mark issue in_progress immediately on spawn to prevent re-spawn. Currently relies on TTL cache which is fragile. The 3 duplicate agents all detected the duplication themselves and reported it, but the damage (wasted capacity) was already done.

title: Redesign coaching metrics + integrate into orch stats + auto-surface at session start
description:
Three-part feature:

## 1. Metric Redesign

ORCHESTRATOR - Strip noise, keep signal:
- KEEP: frame_collapse (delegation rule violation), circular_pattern (contradicting prior knowledge)
- KEEP but tune: behavioral_variation (useful for spawn retry loops)
- KILL: action_ratio (378 fires, wrong signal for orchestrators), analysis_paralysis (352 fires, consecutive bash is normal), compensation_pattern (79 fires, too loose at 30% overlap), context_ratio (unclear value), dylan_signal_prefix (never fired), premise_skipping (never fired)
- ADD: spawn_without_context (no kb context in recent tool window before orch spawn), completion_backlog (agents at Phase:Complete >10 min without orch complete), direct_implementation (Edit/Write on files outside .kb/ .orch/ CLAUDE.md AGENTS.md)

Result: 5 orchestrator metrics (2 kept, 3 new) down from 8 noisy ones.

WORKER - Keep all 4 as-is, let them accumulate real data:
- tool_failure_rate (3+ consecutive failures)
- context_usage (estimated tokens, alert at 80%)
- time_in_phase (minutes since phase change, alert at 15+)
- commit_gap (time since last commit, alert at 30+ min)

## 2. Stats Integration

Add coaching summary section to orch stats output:
```
🧠 BEHAVIORAL HEALTH (coaching plugin, last N days)
  Orchestrator:
    Frame collapse:       3 events (2 sessions)
    Circular patterns:    1 event
    Completion backlog:   2 alerts
    Direct implementation: 0 events
  Workers:
    Context exhaustion:   2 alerts (>80%)
    Tool failure runs:    1 alert
    Stuck in phase:       0 alerts
    Commit gaps:          4 alerts
```

Read from ~/.orch/coaching-metrics.jsonl, aggregate by metric_type for the stats time window.

## 3. Session Start Auto-Surface

Run orch stats --days 1 summary automatically when orchestrator session starts. Could be:
- Plugin hook on first tool call in new session
- session.prompt.before hook if available
- Injection via coaching plugin detecting new orchestrator session

Goal: orchestrator sees system health picture without remembering to run the command.

## Evidence
- coaching-metrics.jsonl has 1000+ entries, dominated by action_ratio (378) and analysis_paralysis (352) noise
- Worker metrics just started working (Feb 14 fix) - context_usage verified
- orch stats already reads events.jsonl, adding coaching-metrics.jsonl pass is natural
- Current session start is skill doc guideline only, not automated

## Key files
- plugins/coaching.ts (metric detection, 1831 lines)
- cmd/orch/stats.go (stats aggregation)
- .opencode/plugin/coaching.ts (deployed copy, must stay in sync)

title: Improve daemon skill inference with description-aware heuristics and accuracy tracking
description: Two gaps in daemon skill inference (pkg/daemon/skill_inference.go): (1) DESCRIPTION-AWARE HEURISTICS: Current inference only uses issue type + skill:* labels + title prefix. It never reads the description. Add keyword/pattern matching on description text to improve inference. Signal words: 'audit/analyze/correlate/investigate/understand/how does' → investigation, 'fix/broken/error/crash/fails' → systematic-debugging (when cause is described) or architect (when vague), 'compare/evaluate/research/best practice' → research. This should be a fallback AFTER label and title checks but BEFORE type-based inference. Keep it simple — keyword matching, not NLP. (2) INFERENCE ACCURACY TRACKING: Add an event type 'spawn.skill_inferred' to events.jsonl that logs {issue_id, inferred_skill, issue_type, title, had_skill_label, had_title_match, used_description_heuristic}. This enables post-hoc analysis of inference quality. Also fix: InferSkillFromTitle still references kb-reflect which is being removed (orch-go-bt7). Key files: pkg/daemon/skill_inference.go (114 lines), pkg/daemon/skill_inference_test.go, pkg/daemon/daemon.go (where inference is called). title: Enrich spawn context with orch tree --cluster for area awareness
description:
Before spawning into an area, the orchestrator should see the full knowledge landscape for that area. This improves skill selection, prompt quality, and prevents duplicate work.

CONTEXT: Currently kb context provides keyword search. orch tree --cluster provides structural awareness — what investigations exist, what decisions were made, what is untested. Together they give the orchestrator full area context before spawning.

SCOPE:
1. When orchestrator runs orch spawn, auto-detect the area from issue labels or task description
2. If area maps to a known cluster, inject orch tree --cluster <area> --depth 3 output into the spawn prompt or show it to orchestrator before spawn confirmation
3. Add orch tree --cluster <area> --format summary flag that produces a 3-5 line area briefing:
   - N investigations, M decisions, K models, J open issues
   - Health smells if any
   - Most recent artifact date
4. Consider adding this to SPAWN_CONTEXT.md template so agents also see where their work fits

DEPENDS ON: health smells and relationship parsing for full value, but basic cluster summary works with current tree.

VERIFICATION:
- orch tree --cluster X --format summary produces concise area briefing
- Spawning into an area with a known cluster surfaces the cluster context
- Agents see where their work fits in the knowledge landscape
- go build ./... succeeds
- go test ./... passes

title: Audit entropy spiral for recoverable features by functional area
description:
## Context
Reverted orch-go to Jan 18 baseline (0bca3dec) after entropy spiral (1163 agent commits, 5.4M LOC churn). Evidence preserved on entropy-spiral-feb2026 branch. Some features built during the spiral were genuinely valuable and should be recovered.

## Approach
Diff Jan 18 (0bca3dec) vs entropy-spiral-feb2026 by functional area. For each area, identify:
- Net-new features (not just churn/refactor cycles)
- Whether the feature compiled/worked at HEAD of spiral branch
- Dependencies on other spiral-era code
- Recovery complexity (clean extract vs needs adaptation)

## Areas to audit
1. **Web UI** - /work-graph route, dashboard agent cards, vite config changes
2. **Spawn system** - Backend abstraction (pkg/spawn/backends/), new flags, spawn pipeline
3. **Completion pipeline** - Pipeline decomposition, skip config, retry logic, SkipConfig type
4. **Daemon** - ProcessedIssueCache, skill inference improvements, processed-cache
5. **Observability** - SSE improvements, attention system (pkg/attention/), churn metrics
6. **Knowledge system** - Models/probes Go code (already recovering separately), kb reflect
7. **CLI commands** - Any new subcommands that were useful (orch phase, orch lint, etc.)

## Deliverables
- Recovery priority list: which features to recover first based on value vs complexity
- For each recommended recovery: exact commits/files to cherry-pick
- Flag features that depend on each other (can't recover X without Y)

## Method
Use git diff, git log, and file analysis on the entropy-spiral-feb2026 branch.
Do NOT modify any code - this is a read-only audit.

title: Recover probe/model/investigation lifecycle decision from entropy spiral branch
description: Dylan's preferred design: 3+ investigations on the same domain warrant creating a model, and from then on probes are made against the model (not standalone investigations). This was a design or decision artifact on the entropy-spiral-feb2026 branch that was lost in the rollback. Agent should search entropy-spiral-feb2026 branch commits for this artifact and recover it. The current decision (2026-02-14-probe-as-universal-evidence-gathering-primitive.md) proposes retiring investigations entirely — Dylan's actual design preserves investigations as the entry point that graduate into models, with probes as the confirmatory mechanism against established models. Key distinction: investigations are for novel exploration, probes are for testing model claims — NOT collapsing both into probes.

title: orch tree: health smell detection with warning badges on clusters
description:
Make orch tree a diagnostic tool by adding health smell detection to clusters.

CONTEXT: orch tree currently renders clusters but gives no signal about which ones need attention. Health smells make the tree a triage tool — the orchestrator sees at a glance where synthesis is needed, where decisions are unacted, where models are untested.

SCOPE:
1. Detect and badge these smells on cluster nodes:
   - ⚠ 15+ investigations without a decision or model = needs synthesis
   - ⚠ Decision without any spawned issues = not acted on
   - ⚠ Model without probes = untested model claims
2. Add --smells flag to filter tree to only clusters with health smells
3. Add smell counts to depth-2 cluster summary line:
   ├─◉ coaching-plugin (15 investigations, 0 decisions) ⚠ needs synthesis
4. Smells should also appear in --format json output

VISUAL FORMAT (from design doc):
├─◉ Entropy Spiral (4 investigations, 2 decisions, 5 issues)  ⚠ health smell
├─◉ Coaching Plugin (15 investigations, 0 decisions) ⚠ needs synthesis
├─◆ Completion Verification (31 investigations, 1 model, 0 probes) ⚠ untested model

DESIGN DOC: docs/designs/2026-02-14-knowledge-lineage-tree-visualization.md (health smells section, line 339)
EXISTING CODE: pkg/tree/ (Phase 1a + 1b renderer, cluster detection, KnowledgeNode struct)

VERIFICATION:
- orch tree --smells shows only clusters with health smell badges
- orch tree --depth 2 shows smell counts on cluster summary lines
- orch tree --format json includes smell metadata per cluster
- Smell thresholds: 15+ investigations/no decision, decision/no issues, model/no probes
- go build ./... succeeds
- go test ./... passes

title: Fix daemon duplicate spawn: issue spawned 10 times in 20 minutes while orchestrator was present
description:
LIVE INCIDENT (Feb 14): orch-go-w50 was spawned 10 times by the daemon in ~20 minutes. All 10 agents did the same work. Issue remained OPEN status with triage:ready label, so the daemon kept picking it up every poll cycle.

Evidence (from orch status --all):
  orch-go-rpj   Extract rework expla...   Complete     4m
  orch-go-12a   Extract rework expla...   Complete     29m
  orch-go-0b1   Extract rework expla...   Planning     1m  (still running at time of detection)
  orch-go-7q8   Extract rework expla...   Complete     18m
  orch-go-vxh   Extract rework expla...   Complete     10m
  orch-go-og3   Extract rework expla...   Complete     23m
  orch-go-bga   Extract rework expla...   Complete     7m
  orch-go-es6   Extract rework expla...   Complete     13m
  orch-go-as3   Extract rework expla...   Complete     25m
  orch-go-c91   Extract rework expla...   Complete     20m

Root cause: daemon dedup cache (TTL-based) is not preventing respawns. The daemon polls bd ready, sees orch-go-w50 is still open+triage:ready, and spawns again. Each spawn gets a unique workspace name so dedup doesnt match.

Cost: ~80K tokens wasted (10 agents x ~8K avg), ~2-3% weekly API budget burned for nothing.

Immediate fix applied: bd update orch-go-w50 --status in_progress (stops daemon from picking it up).

Required fix: daemon must track which beads IDs it has already spawned agents for and refuse to spawn duplicates. The SpawnedIssueTracker already exists but clearly isnt working. This is the same bug documented in .kb/models/daemon-autonomous-operation/probes/2026-02-14-daemon-duplicate-spawn-ttl-fragility.md

This is P0 because it actively burns API budget and creates confusion every time the daemon runs.
close_reason: Phase: Complete - Fixed daemon duplicate spawn by changing UpdateBeadsStatus failure handling from silent-continue to fail-fast, preventing spawns without persistent tracking

title: Extract spawn gates from spawn_cmd.go to pkg/spawn/gates/
description:
## Goal

Strangler fig extraction: pull 3 gate concerns out of spawn_cmd.go (2,332 lines) into pkg/spawn/gates/. This creates attractor packages so future agents naturally route spawn-gate code there instead of spawn_cmd.go.

## What to extract

1. **hotspot.go** — hotspot analysis/checking logic (currently inline in spawn_cmd.go)
2. **concurrency.go** — concurrency limit checking, capacity management
3. **triage.go** — triage bypass logic, label checking

## Pattern

For each concern:
1. Create pkg/spawn/gates/{concern}.go with the extracted logic
2. spawn_cmd.go calls gates.CheckHotspot(), gates.CheckConcurrency(), etc.
3. Tests move/create in pkg/spawn/gates/{concern}_test.go

## Constraints

- Do NOT refactor spawn_cmd.go beyond what's needed for extraction
- Do NOT change behavior — pure structural extraction
- Each extracted file should be focused and small (<200 lines)
- spawn_cmd.go should shrink by the extracted amount

## Verification

- go build ./cmd/orch/ passes
- go test ./cmd/orch/... passes
- go test ./pkg/spawn/gates/... passes
- spawn_cmd.go line count decreased

title: orch clean --sessions deletes active TUI/interactive sessions idle >5min
description:
Problem: cleanUntrackedDiskSessions() in clean_cmd.go:408-539 deletes sessions not tracked by any .orch/workspace/*/session_id file. TUI/interactive sessions have no workspace, so they bypass Layer 1 protection entirely. If user hasnt sent a message in >5min, Layer 2 (recency) marks session as orphaned and deletes it.

Code path: orch clean --sessions -> cleanUntrackedDiskSessions() -> !trackedSessionIDs[session.ID] (TUI has no workspace, always true) -> now.Sub(updatedAt) > 5min (user paused, true) -> skips IsSessionProcessing (only checked for recent sessions) -> client.DeleteSession (SESSION DELETED).

Fix: Call IsSessionProcessing() for ALL untracked sessions before deletion, not just recently active ones. Cost: one API call per untracked session. Minimal fix using existing Layer 3 defense thats currently short-circuited.

Evidence: Model .kb/models/session-deletion-vectors.md (Vector #2). Investigation .kb/investigations/2026-02-13-inv-verify-whether-orch-clean-kills-headless-sessions.md. Primary source: cmd/orch/clean_cmd.go:464-485.

Files: cmd/orch/clean_cmd.go lines 408-539.

title: Agents in other projects fail trying to create cross-repo beads issues
description: Problem: Agents running in project A (e.g., price-watch) discover issues that belong in project B (e.g., orch-go) and try to run 'bd create' with a different workdir. This fails with 'ENOENT: posix_spawn /bin/zsh' because the agent's shell sandbox can't change to another project directory. This is recurring — any agent that discovers a systemic/orchestration issue hits this. Current workaround: agent dumps the bd create command for the human/orchestrator to run manually, but this is fragile and loses issues. Solution options: 1) Add explicit guidance in orchestrator skill and worker-base skill for cross-repo issue creation — agent should output a structured 'CROSS_REPO_ISSUE' block that the orchestrator can pick up and create. 2) Add 'bd create --repo orch-go' support so agents can create issues in other repos without changing workdir. 3) At minimum, update worker skill guidance so agents don't waste attempts and instead cleanly hand off the issue details. Evidence: price-watch orchestrator failed trying to create orch-go-ga6 (push batching issue). Pattern is recurring per user report.

title: Architect: Design accretion gravity enforcement infrastructure
description:
## Problem

Accretion Gravity is a proven principle (spawn_cmd.go grew from reasonable to 2,332 lines as 25+ agents each added features where similar code existed), but it has ZERO enforcement infrastructure. It's a reminder, not a gate — violating Gate Over Remind.

The principle identifies structural constraints as the fix:
- Explicit boundaries in CLAUDE.md ("new logic goes in pkg/, not cmd/")
- File-level rules ("if adding >10 lines to X, create a new package")
- Architectural review at completion, not just functional review

But none of these exist as infrastructure yet. Agents still freely accrete.

## What Needs Designing

Design the enforcement mechanisms that make Accretion Gravity a gate, not a reminder. Consider:

1. **Detection**: How do we detect accretion in real-time (not post-mortem)?
   - File size thresholds in completion verification?
   - Line-count delta checks in pre-commit hooks?
   - Hotspot analysis already exists but only warns

2. **Prevention**: How do we prevent accretion before it happens?
   - CLAUDE.md boundaries that agents actually respect?
   - Spawn context that includes "DO NOT add to these files" directives?
   - File-level extraction rules?

3. **Enforcement**: Where do gates live?
   - Completion verification (orch complete)?
   - Pre-commit hooks?
   - Coaching plugin real-time detection?
   - Spawn-time directives?

4. **Integration**: How does this compose with existing infrastructure?
   - Hotspot analysis (already detects bloated files)
   - Coaching plugin (already injects real-time feedback)
   - Completion verification (already gates on deliverables)
   - Code extraction patterns guide (already documents how to extract)

## Deliverable

Investigation with concrete design recommendations. NOT implementation — just the architecture of enforcement.

## Evidence

- spawn_cmd.go: 2,332 lines (CRITICAL hotspot)
- session.go: 2,166 lines (CRITICAL hotspot)
- doctor.go: 1,912 lines (CRITICAL hotspot)
- coaching.ts: 1,830 lines (CRITICAL hotspot)
- Principle provenance: .kb/decisions/2026-02-12-strangler-fig-refactor-cmd-orch.md
- Existing guide: .kb/guides/code-extraction-patterns.md

title: Rework daemon: no auto-close, faster polling, review-queue model
description:
Two changes to daemon behavior based on verifiability-first design session:

1. DAEMON NEVER AUTO-CLOSES ISSUES
Current: daemon polls for Phase:Complete, verifies artifacts, closes issue automatically.
New: daemon detects Phase:Complete, flags agent as 'ready for review', but does NOT close the issue. Dylan closes issues through the orchestrator after two-gate verification (comprehension + behavioral). Daemon pauses spawning when N agents are waiting for review (default 3) so work doesnt pile up unboundedly.

This means the daemon loop becomes:
  - Poll bd ready, spawn within capacity
  - Monitor for Phase:Complete
  - When agents complete, mark as ready-for-review (not closed)
  - Pause spawning when N agents waiting for review
  - Dylan reviews batch through orchestrator, closes each
  - Daemon sees capacity freed, spawns more

2. FASTER POLL INTERVAL
Current: 60s poll interval feels sluggish. Dashboard doesnt update quickly after CLI commands.
New: Drop poll interval to 15-20s. The daemon is just doing lightweight beads queries - this is not a meaningful load increase. Also reduce spawn delay from 10s to 3-5s (dedup cache already prevents duplicates).

Why both together: the faster polling makes the system feel responsive when Dylan is actively working. The no-auto-close ensures faster polling doesnt mean faster unverified closures.

REMOVES: daemon auto-completion loop (or repurpose it to only flag ready-for-review, not close)
KEEPS: VerificationTracker pause logic (but now its 'pause after N ready-for-review' not 'pause after N auto-completions')
KEEPS: orch daemon resume command

Prior work: 85a0e021 (verification tracker), design session Feb 14 (verifiability-first constraint)
Supersedes the auto-completion behavior from the current daemon.go completion loop.

title: Update skill system for verifiability-first: explain-back scaffolding and human_verified gate
description:
Update the orchestrator skill (skills/src/meta/orchestrator/) to implement completion review as the primary orchestrator workflow.

## What Completion Review Is

Completion review is the two-gate process Dylan uses to engage with agent output. Every completed agent goes through this before closing. This is the orchestrator's primary job — not dispatching work, but understanding what was done.

### Gate 1: Comprehension (Explain-Back)

The orchestrator presents 'the what' — factual scaffolding about what was built/found/decided, in plain language. Dylan explains 'the so-what' — why it matters, what it changes, what concerns him.

Design principles:
- Safe to be wrong (Dylan should feel comfortable saying 'I don't get it')
- Not starting from nothing (orchestrator provides the what)
- Not given the answer (orchestrator does NOT present the so-what)
- No sycophancy: do NOT mirror Dylan's words back, do NOT validate incomplete understanding, do NOT say 'great explanation' when the answer is partial
- If Dylan's explanation misses something important, say so directly
- If Dylan asks a question that reveals genuine understanding, that counts

### Gate 2: Behavioral (Tiered by Work Type)

After comprehension passes:

| Type | Behavioral Gate |
|------|----------------|
| Features | Run it, see it work |
| Bug fixes | Reproduce bug, confirm fixed |  
| Investigations | Spot-check one claim against reality |
| Decisions | N/A (comprehension IS the gate) |

### Pacing

Not every completion review is heavy. Friction scales with complexity/risk:
- Light completions (typo, config, formatting): fast explain-back, minimal behavioral
- Medium completions (single feature, known pattern): standard explain-back, run it
- Heavy completions (architectural change, new subsystem, design decision): thorough explain-back, careful behavioral verification

### Anti-Patterns to Prevent

- Rubber-stamping: orchestrator accepting 'yeah that makes sense' without probing
- Sycophancy: 'Great insight!' when Dylan gives a surface-level answer  
- Ceremony theater: going through the motions on trivial completions
- Skipping comprehension on investigations/decisions because 'artifact is deliverable'
- Presenting options then asking 'which would you pick?' (that's giving the answer)

### Integration Points

- The orchestrator skill's SYNTHESIZE section needs to be rewritten around completion review
- The 'Investigation Outcomes' section needs to include spot-check behavioral gate
- The autonomy section needs to clarify: orchestrator acts autonomously on dispatching, but NEVER skips completion review
- orch complete CLI: --explain flag accepts the explain-back summary (conversational, not CLI prompts)

### What to Modify

Primary file: the orchestrator skill source in skills/src/meta/orchestrator/
Specifically the SYNTHESIZE section, completion review protocol, and investigation outcomes.

Also update worker-base shared skill to require plain-language summary in SYNTHESIS.md (this becomes the scaffolding for explain-back).

### References

- kb-158420 (human verification required)
- kb-320fb2 (explain-back gate) 
- kb-afa611 (sycophancy constraint)
- kb-d4e04d (explain-back is conversational not CLI)
- kb-286915 (completion review model: all types get comprehension gate)
- kb-853ff6 (fail-fast constraint on daemon spawn prerequisites)
- .kb/decisions/2026-02-14-verifiability-first-hard-constraint.md

title: Redesign control plane circuit breaker heuristics - daily commit count is wrong signal
description: The circuit breaker fired on its first day (59/20 commits) during a normal batch review session. The threshold of 20 commits/day is too aggressive for normal operation (3-4 agents produce this easily). The entropy spiral was 45 commits/day sustained for 26 days — the signal isn't 'too many commits today' but 'too many commits sustained over multiple days without human verification.' The heuristic needs to detect sustained unverified velocity, not single-day spikes. Consider: rolling window (3-day average), human-verified vs agent-only commit distinction, commits-without-human-interaction metric, or graduated warnings before halt. Also: the post-commit hook counts ALL commits including orchestrator cherry-picks from agent workspaces, inflating the count.

title: Daemon behavior change: pause after N completions without human verification
description: Current daemon behavior: polls bd ready, auto-spawns indefinitely. This produced the 26-day spiral (1163 commits, 24/7 autonomous operation, zero human involvement). New behavior: daemon tracks completions since last human verification. After N completions without human activity, daemon PAUSES and waits. Not alerts - actually stops spawning. Dylan explicitly resumes when he is back and has reviewed completed work. This is the mechanical enforcement of the verifiability-first constraint. Design considerations: (1) What counts as human activity? Explain-back in orch complete conversation, not just a CLI flag. (2) What is N? Start conservative (e.g., 3 completions). (3) Daemon should surface what is waiting for review when Dylan returns. Prior art: every post-mortem recommended this, none implemented it.
notes:
DESIGN SESSION CONTEXT (Feb 14):

Dylan's insight: daemon pause isn't just safety - it's a workflow design tool. Batching completions into review sessions means: sit down, review N things with two-gate verification (comprehension + behavioral), release next batch, walk away. Sustainable rhythm, not constant availability.

Connects to two-gate verification (orch-go-tyi): daemon pauses after N completions. When Dylan returns, he reviews each completion through comprehension gate (what/so-what) then behavioral gate (tiered). Only after verification does system proceed.

Dylan accepts the slowdown if it means quality and understanding.

title: Investigate: which skills produce investigation artifacts and should they?
description:
## Problem

936 investigations exist, only 3 synthesized (312:1 ratio). 45 created in February alone. Investigation production far outpaces synthesis capacity.

Hypothesis: too many skills are configured to produce .kb/investigations/ artifacts when they shouldn't need to. Investigation files should be reserved for genuine exploratory/understanding work, not as a default output for every skill.

## What to check

1. Audit all skill definitions in ~/.claude/skills/ — which ones list investigation artifacts as outputs/deliverables?
2. For each skill that produces investigations: is that appropriate, or should the skill produce a different artifact (or none)?
3. Check skill.yaml or SKILL.md `outputs.required` fields across all skills
4. Check spawn context generation — does it default to investigation deliverables?

## Also check

- Are agents creating investigations opportunistically even when not asked to? (e.g., feature-impl agents creating investigations as side effects)
- Is the investigation template/path being injected into spawn context unnecessarily?

## Deliverable

List of skills that produce investigations, recommendation for which should stop, and what they should produce instead (if anything).

## Evidence

- 936 total investigations, 3 synthesized, 72 archived
- 45 investigations in February 2026 alone
- kb reflect shows 29 synthesis opportunities across 12+ clusters

title: Probe: do agents naturally route to extracted packages without being told?
description:
## Hypothesis

After extracting spawn gates to pkg/spawn/gates/, agents given spawn-gate-related tasks will naturally find and add code to the extracted packages — NOT to spawn_cmd.go — without being told where to put it.

## Protocol

1. Wait for extraction (orch-go extract spawn gates issue) to complete
2. Spawn a feature-impl agent with a task like "add rate-limit gate to spawn" or "add file-size pre-check to spawn"
3. CRITICAL: Do NOT mention pkg/spawn/gates/ in the spawn context. Let the agent discover the codebase.
4. After completion, check: where did the agent add code?

## Success criteria

- Agent found pkg/spawn/gates/ and added code there → CONFIRMS hypothesis
- Agent added to spawn_cmd.go anyway → CONTRADICTS hypothesis, need stronger signals

## What to record

- Which files the agent created/modified (git diff)
- Whether the agent mentioned discovering pkg/spawn/gates/ in its reasoning
- Total lines added to spawn_cmd.go vs pkg/spawn/gates/

## Constraint

This is an ORCHESTRATOR probe — the orchestrator runs this manually after extraction completes. Do NOT spawn this as autonomous work. The value is in observing agent behavior, not automating the observation.

title: Architect: Knowledge lineage tree visualization for dashboard and orchestrator
description:
Design a tree-based knowledge lineage visualization that shows the natural parent-child relationships between knowledge artifacts (investigations, decisions, models, probes, guides) and the beads issues they spawn.

The tree structure falls out naturally from existing data:
- Investigations reference prior work (Prior-Work table)
- Decisions cite investigations (Evidence section)
- Issues have descriptions pointing to decisions
- Probes live inside model directories (filesystem hierarchy)
- Models synthesize from investigations (Synthesized From)

Example rendering (Unicode box-drawing tree):

orch-go knowledge tree
│
├─◉ Entropy Spiral
│ ├─◉ post-mortems/2026-01-02-system-spiral-dec27-jan02.md
│ ├─◉ investigations/2026-02-12-inv-entropy-spiral-postmortem.md
│ ├─◉ investigations/2026-02-14-inv-entropy-spiral-deep-analysis.md
│ │ ├─★ decisions/2026-02-14-verifiability-first-hard-constraint.md
│ │ │ ├─● orch-go-5sc  Decision record                    CLOSED
│ │ │ ├─● orch-go-7jl  Daemon pause after N completions   CLOSED
│ │ │ └─◇ orch-go-6th  Skill system update                triage:review
│ │ └─● orch-go-agr  Trajectory audit                     IN PROGRESS
│ └─◉ handoffs/2026-02-13-entropy-spiral-recovery.md

DESIGN QUESTIONS:
1. Where does this render? Dashboard (Svelte, persistent, browsable), orchestrator session (inline, contextual during verification), or both?
2. Data model: How to extract the tree from existing artifacts? Parse Prior-Work tables, Synthesized From headers, beads deps, filesystem hierarchy? Or add explicit lineage metadata?
3. Interaction: Click to expand/collapse? Filter by area? Highlight verification state (verified vs pending)? Show health smells (15 investigations with no decision = cluster smell)?
4. Integration with verify flow: When daemon pauses and Dylan reviews, show the tree with new nodes highlighted so he sees where work fits before explain-back begins.
5. CLI vs web: Should 'orch tree' or 'kb tree' exist as CLI command (for orchestrator session rendering) in addition to dashboard?
6. Grouping: The top-level clusters (Entropy Spiral, Coaching Plugin, etc.) - are these inferred from area: labels, filesystem paths, or explicit topic tags?

USE CASES:
- Session start: see the knowledge landscape, find where to focus
- Triage: spot clusters with many investigations but no decisions (smell)
- Verification: see where a completed deliverable fits before explain-back
- Orientation: new session Claude sees the tree, immediately understands project structure

CONSTRAINTS:
- Must work with existing .kb/ file structure and beads issue data
- Tree relationships already implicit in artifacts - design should extract, not require manual curation
- Dylan doesnt read Go - the tree IS his primary interface for understanding project state

Prior art: The tree rendering above was produced in conversation and Dylan said 'wow, this is a really cool view' - that specific format resonated.

title: Daemon: auto-spawn extraction before feature work on CRITICAL hotspots
description:
## Goal

When daemon processes a triage:ready issue that targets a CRITICAL hotspot file (>1,500 lines), automatically spawn an extraction agent first to create the attractor package, then spawn the feature agent.

## How it works

1. Daemon picks up issue from triage:ready queue
2. Daemon runs hotspot check (already exists) against issue description/title to infer target files
3. If target file is CRITICAL (>1,500 lines): spawn extraction agent first, block feature spawn until extraction completes
4. If target file is fine: spawn feature agent normally

## Extraction agent task

"Extract [inferred concern] from [file] into [pkg/appropriate/package/]. Pure structural extraction — no behavior changes."

The extraction creates the attractor. Then the feature agent discovers it naturally (proven by probe orch-go-8ao: agent found pkg/spawn/gates/ without being told).

## Evidence

- Probe confirmed: agents naturally route to extracted packages (orch-go-8ao)
- spawn_cmd.go shrank from 2,332 → 1,973 lines across two extractions
- Agent even pulled existing inline code into the attractor package

## Constraints

- Don't over-engineer the file targeting — title/description keyword matching is fine for v1
- Extraction agent should be small-scoped (one concern at a time)
- If hotspot detection can't determine target file, skip and spawn normally

title: Architect: Probe as primary evidence artifact — retire investigations
description: Dylan values the probe concept because it forces specificity: 'test this claim' vs 'look into this area'. Investigations feel like one-offs disconnected from models, while probes create granularity useful for thinking ('what probes do we need before deciding?'). But the current system creates friction: Dylan has to decide 'investigation or probe?' before requesting evidence-gathering, and the routing logic is split across orchestrator skill (spawn-time detection) and investigation skill (mode detection from SPAWN_CONTEXT markers). Design question: should probes become the universal evidence-gathering primitive, with investigations retired? Key constraints: (1) probes currently must target a model — would need to support targeting any claim source (model, decision, assumption, code), (2) probes currently live nested under models (.kb/models/{name}/probes/) — may need their own location for discoverability, (3) investigation skill has significant infrastructure (D.E.K.N., Prior Work tables, self-review) that probes lack, (4) ~800+ existing investigations in .kb/investigations/ need migration/coexistence strategy. Dylan's framing: 'I should be thinking about what I want to understand, not which artifact shape to use.' The routing decision should be invisible to the meta-orchestrator.
close_reason: Phase: Complete - Decision record created at .kb/decisions/2026-02-14-probe-as-universal-evidence-gathering-primitive.md. title: Architect: design language-agnostic accretion metrics for cross-project orchestration
description:
## Problem

Accretion gravity enforcement (file size thresholds, hotspot detection, completion gates) is currently hardcoded for orch-go — a Go project with specific structure. We want these metrics available for ANY project we orchestrate, regardless of language (Go, TypeScript, Python, Rust, Svelte, etc.) or file structure conventions.

## Key Design Questions

1. **Threshold calibration:** Are 800/1500 line thresholds universal, or do they need to be language-relative? (A 1500-line Python file is very different from a 1500-line Go file with verbose error handling. A 200-line Svelte component might be bloated while a 200-line Go file is fine.)

2. **What counts as a 'source file'?** Generated files (.svelte-kit/), test files (*_test.go), config files, vendored deps — how do we filter these language-agnostically?

3. **Project configuration:** How does a project declare its accretion policy? Per-repo config file? Convention-based defaults? Language auto-detection?

4. **Metric collection:** The accretion.delta event in orch complete uses git diff + wc -l — this is language-agnostic already. What else is needed?

5. **Integration with existing infrastructure:** orch hotspot, spawn gates, completion gates, coaching plugin — which parts are already portable vs orch-go specific?

## Evidence (orch-go stats as baseline)

Current orch-go file size distribution (490 source files, 154K total lines):
- 0-100 lines: 141 files (28.8%)
- 100-300 lines: 170 files (34.7%)  — median is 205 lines
- 300-500 lines: 93 files (19.0%)
- 500-800 lines: 42 files (8.6%)
- 800-1500 lines: 31 files (6.3%) — WARNING zone
- 1500+ lines: 13 files (2.7%) — CRITICAL zone

Top offenders: spawn_cmd.go (2173), doctor.go (1909), complete_cmd.go (1847), coaching.ts (1830), status_cmd.go (1625)

## Constraint

This is a design/architecture question. Produce recommendations, not implementation. Consider what a .orch/accretion.yaml or similar config might look like.

## Prior Knowledge

- Current enforcement: .kb/investigations/2026-02-14-inv-architect-design-accretion-gravity-enforcement.md (four-layer architecture, hardcoded 800/1500 thresholds)
- kn decision: Accretion gravity fix is structural extraction (strangler fig), not detection/warnings
- Accretion metric already captured: accretion.delta events in orch complete track file-level lines added/removed
close_reason: Designed language-agnostic accretion metrics: confirmed uniform 800/1500 thresholds via cross-project spike (Go/TS), designed 4 metric primitives (risk_file_ratio, extraction_debt, accretion_velocity, file_bloat_score), enriched event schema with project_dir for cross-project aggregation, --all-projects flag for orch hotspot, generated file exclusion patterns. No discovered work.

title: Orchestrator pushes after every agent completion instead of batching coherent changesets
description: Problem: AGENTS.md says 'Work is NOT complete until git push succeeds' but this is session-end guidance, not per-commit. Current orchestrator behavior is to push after every orch complete, which deploys partial infrastructure that can't function yet (e.g., pushing OauthToken model without ScsOauthClient that uses it). The right workflow is: build a coherent chain of changes, verify locally, push, verify in production. Need guidance in the orchestrator skill or AGENTS.md that distinguishes session-end push from mid-session commits that should batch before pushing. Solution: Add push strategy guidance — push after a coherent set of commits that can be verified end-to-end, not after every individual agent completion. Evidence: In price-watch, orchestrator repeatedly tried to push after completing pw-8d6u (OauthToken model) before the rest of the API collector chain was built. Repro: Complete any agent in a multi-agent chain and observe orchestrator attempting to push before dependent work is done.

title: Add completion_backlog metric detection in Go backend (serve_agents.go)
description:
Phase 6 of coaching metrics redesign (see .kb/investigations/2026-02-14-design-coaching-metrics-redesign.md). Depends on Phase 1 (shared metrics writer).

Detect agents at Phase:Complete for >10 minutes without orch complete being run. Write completion_backlog metric to ~/.orch/coaching-metrics.jsonl from Go side.

Implementation: piggyback on /api/agents handler in cmd/orch/serve_agents.go (already polled by dashboard every 30s):
- When serving agent list, check for agents where Phase == 'Complete' and completedAt + 10min < now
- Write metric using shared pkg/coaching writer
- Include session_id and agent beads_id in metric details

This is the first metric written by Go backend rather than TypeScript plugin. JSONL is append-only and safe for concurrent writers.

Note from design: 'breaks the plugin is the only metric writer pattern' — but the alternative (plugin polling agent status) creates circular dependency. Go backend has the data, so Go writes the metric.

title: Add accretion metric to orch complete: track file growth/shrinkage per agent
description:
## Goal

During orch complete, analyze the agent's git diff and log which files grew vs shrank. Over time this tells us whether accretion gravity is inverting.

## Metric to capture

For each file in the diff:
- file path
- lines added / lines removed
- net delta
- whether file is >800 lines (accretion risk zone)

Log to ~/.orch/events.jsonl as an accretion.delta event type.

## Future use

- Dashboard can show "lines added to files >800 lines" trend over time
- Alerts when monolithic files are growing despite extraction efforts
- Proves or disproves that strangler fig strategy is working

## Priority

P2 — not urgent, but the sooner we have data the sooner we can measure. Create after probe confirms the overall strategy works.

title: orch clean can't purge ghost registry entries from derived state sources
description: Problem: After agents die (from orch complete Overmind cascade, missing projects, etc), orch status shows zombie entries indefinitely. orch clean handles disk state (OpenCode sessions, workspaces, investigation files) but the 4 derived state sources (OpenCode sessions, tmux windows, beads issues, workspaces) still produce ghost entries. Examples from today: pw-8966 (project not on disk), pw-8975, orch-go-y55 (already completed), untracked-Feb12-2144, untracked-Feb12-2219, untracked-Feb13-0933 — all showing as 'Active: 8' despite zero live agents. orch abandon fails on cross-project and untracked IDs. Solution: orch clean needs a --ghosts or --registry-purge flag that identifies entries with no live tmux window, no live OpenCode session, and no active beads issue, then removes them from the derived state. Or orch status needs to filter out entries that have no live backing source. Evidence: After running orch clean --all today, 8 ghost entries persisted. Zero tmux windows, zero live OpenCode sessions, but orch status still reports Active: 8.
close_reason: Fixed ghost agents inflating Active count (8→3) via two phantom detection bugs + added orch clean --ghosts. Tests: go test ./cmd/orch/ - PASS (2.181s). No discovered work.

title: orch tree Phase 1b: relationship parsing, decision/model nodes, and work view
description:
Phase 1a (orch-go-3wu) shipped the tree renderer and filesystem-based clustering. But the core value — the relationship graph where decisions hang off investigations and issues hang off decisions — is missing.

WHAT IS MISSING (from Dylan review of Phase 1a):
1. No relationship parsing — investigations are flat lists, not linked via Prior-Work tables or Evidence sections
2. No decisions or models in tree — only investigations from synthesized/ directories appear
3. Work view is empty — orch tree --work renders nothing
4. Uncategorized is a black hole — 800+ investigations not in synthesized/ subdirs dumped there
5. Default should be --depth 2 (full tree is 1,129 lines, unusable)

SCOPE:
1. Parse Prior-Work tables in investigations to build parent-child edges
2. Parse Evidence sections in decisions to link decisions → investigations
3. Parse Synthesized From headers in models to link models → investigations
4. Add .kb/decisions/*.md as ★ decision nodes in the tree
5. Add .kb/models/*/ as ◆ model nodes with probes as children
6. Link beads issues to decisions/investigations via description references
7. Implement orch tree --work (issues grouped by state with knowledge provenance)
8. Change default depth to 2 (--depth flag still overrides)

TARGET OUTPUT (from design doc — this is what "wow" looked like):

orch-go knowledge tree
│
├─◉ Entropy Spiral
│ ├─◉ investigations/2026-02-14-inv-entropy-spiral-deep-analysis.md
│ │ ├─★ decisions/2026-02-14-verifiability-first-hard-constraint.md
│ │ │ ├─● orch-go-5sc  Decision record                    CLOSED
│ │ │ └─◇ orch-go-6th  Skill system update                triage:review
│ │ └─● orch-go-agr  Trajectory audit                     IN PROGRESS

orch-go work tree
│
├─⚡ NEEDS VERIFICATION (3)
│ ├─● orch-go-7jl  Daemon pause after N completions
│ │ └─ from ★ verifiability-first-hard-constraint
│ │    └─ from ◉ entropy-spiral-deep-analysis

EXISTING CODE: pkg/tree/ (from Phase 1a — renderer, cluster detection, KnowledgeNode struct)
DESIGN DOC: docs/designs/2026-02-14-knowledge-lineage-tree-visualization.md (full extraction algorithm, data model, relationship sources table at line 66)

VERIFICATION:
- orch tree --depth 2 shows clusters with decision/model counts
- orch tree --cluster entropy-spiral shows investigation→decision→issue lineage
- orch tree --work shows issues grouped by state with provenance trails
- Decisions and models appear as ★ and ◆ nodes (not just investigations)
- Default output is --depth 2 (not 1,129 lines)
- go build ./... succeeds
- go test ./... passes

title: Design solution for model artifact staleness — models drift from code
description: Recurring problem: .kb/models/ artifacts describe system behavior but don't get updated when code changes. Examples: agent-lifecycle-state-model.md still referenced registry after pkg/registry/ was deleted (fixed manually during Feb 13 refactor). Models describe code paths, invariants, and constraints that become stale as implementation evolves. The probe system was designed to test model claims, but probes are manual and reactive — they don't catch drift from routine code changes. Need architectural decision on how to keep models accurate. Options might include: (1) code-change hooks that flag potentially-affected models, (2) model-code linkage metadata that enables staleness detection, (3) periodic model validation as part of completion workflow, (4) acceptance that models are approximate and only update during synthesis. Prior art: .kb/decisions/2026-01-12-models-as-understanding-artifacts.md, .kb/models/PHASE4_REVIEW.md, constraint 'D.E.K.N. Next field must be updated when marking Status: Complete'.
close_reason: Phase: Complete - Produced decision record (.kb/decisions/2026-02-14-model-staleness-detection.md) recommending Detect-Annotate-Queue approach for model staleness: git-diff-based detection at spawn time with staleness annotations for agents and periodic kb reflect sweeps. Investigation documents 5 findings including 12+ stale file references across 24 models. 3-phase implementation plan: (1) formalize code_refs format, (2) spawn-time detection in kbcontext.go, (3) kb reflect --type model-drift. Created: orch-go-bm9 (backfill code_refs), orch-go-2qj (spawn-time detection), orch-go-fq5 (kb reflect integration). Cross-repo: 1 CROSS_REPO_ISSUE block below.

title: Fix spawn template: remove default investigation deliverable from all spawns
description:
## Problem

pkg/spawn/context.go:203-226 tells ALL worker spawns to create investigation files. This is the root cause of the 312:1 investigation-to-synthesis ratio (936 investigations, 3 synthesized).

Only 5-7 skills should produce investigations as primary deliverables: investigation, architect, research, codebase-audit, reliability-testing. Feature-impl only when --phases includes investigation.

## Fix

1. Remove investigation deliverable setup from default spawn template (context.go:203-226)
2. Make investigation production skill-specific — only inject investigation deliverable for skills that should produce them
3. Expected result: ~70% reduction in investigation file creation

## Evidence

- Root cause analysis: .kb/investigations/2026-02-14-inv-investigate-skills-produce-investigation-artifacts.md
- 936 total investigations, 3 synthesized, 45 in February alone
- Feature-impl agents for bug fixes creating unnecessary investigations (og-feat-fix-claude-md, og-feat-implement-kb-reflect, etc.)

## Scope

Surgical fix to context.go spawn template. Do NOT refactor the entire spawn system — just gate the investigation deliverable on skill type.

title: Completion flow: add explain-back verification step to orch complete
description: Current orch complete checks agent self-reported success (Phase:Complete, tests pass, deliverables exist). Missing: human comprehension verification. New step in orch complete: after showing synthesis/results, system asks Dylan to explain what was built and why in his own words. His explanation is captured as a beads comment or artifact. This creates: (1) An unfakeable verification gate - cant rubber-stamp a conversational explanation. (2) A comprehension record for future sessions - next Claude can read Dylans understanding. (3) The distinction between agent-done and human-verified. Design: Could be conversational (orchestrator asks, Dylan answers) or structured (fill in: what it does, why it matters, how you verified). The explain-back IS the gate - completion doesnt close until its provided. Analogy: pilot callouts in instrument flying - forces processing, not just glancing.
notes:
DESIGN SESSION OUTCOME (Feb 14):

Two-gate verification model:
1. COMPREHENSION GATE (what/so-what):
   - Orchestrator presents 'the what' - factual scaffolding of what was built
   - Dylan explains 'the so-what' - what it means for his workflow, what worries him
   - Design principles: safe to be wrong, not starting from nothing, not given the answer
   - The split: AI provides the what, human provides the so-what

2. BEHAVIORAL GATE (tiered by deliverable type):
   - Feature: run the command, see the result
   - Bug fix: reproduce the bug, confirm it is gone
   - Refactor: run tests, basic smoke test
   - Investigation/decision: no behavioral gate needed - artifact IS the deliverable

Order: comprehension first, then behavioral. No point watching something work if you dont understand what its supposed to do.

Key design constraint from Dylan: system cant do the thinking for him. Leading questions that Dylan just confirms = rubber-stamping with extra steps. The so-what must be genuinely Dylans articulation.

Tested live in session: used orch-go-66j (skill-class awareness gate fix) as test case. Dylan confirmed the interaction felt right - made him think but not too much.

title: Design session: structural solutions to accretion gravity in agentic coding
description:
## Problem (Systemic)

Accretion gravity is a fundamental constraint of task-scoped agents: agents add code where similar code exists because that's their only signal about where code belongs. No agent will spontaneously create a new package — that's not in their task.

The prior architect session (orch-go-4mu) designed 4 detection/warning layers (CLAUDE.md, spawn gates, completion gates, coaching). But detection without direction creates stuck agents. The principle says "structural constraints, not better agents" — and all 4 layers try to make better-informed agents.

## The Gap

None of the proposed solutions tell agents WHERE to put code. They only say "don't add here." An agent told "don't add to spawn_cmd.go" still doesn't know that pkg/spawn/gates/hotspot.go is the right target.

## Questions to Explore

1. **Why do agents accrete?** Because file proximity is their only architectural signal. What other signals could exist?
2. **What would a structural fix look like?** Not detection — actual constraints that make accretion physically harder than the alternative.
3. **Is extraction-first viable?** Before adding features to bloated files, could we spawn extraction first to create landing zones?
4. **Can spawn context carry architectural intent?** Orchestrator decides "this code goes in pkg/X/Y.go" at spawn time — agent doesn't choose.
5. **What do other agentic coding systems do?** Is this a solved problem anywhere?
6. **Does package structure itself prevent accretion?** If pkg/spawn/gates/ exists with clear purpose, do agents naturally use it?

## What This Is NOT

- NOT another detection/warning system
- NOT CLAUDE.md rules agents will ignore under pressure
- NOT gates that fire after the damage is done

## Constraint

This is a design session for Dylan + orchestrator, not a spawnable investigation. The solution needs to be general enough to apply to any agentic coding system, not just orch-go.
notes:
## Design Session Outcome (Feb 14 2026)

Rejected architect's 4-layer detection/warning approach. Detection without direction creates stuck agents.

**Actual solution: Invert the gravity field.**

1. Extract concerns from monolithic files into focused packages (strangler fig)
2. Agents naturally discover and route to focused packages via search (proven by probe orch-go-8ao)
3. Monolithic files shrink as new work routes to attractors AND agents pull existing code into them

**Probe evidence:** Agent given "add rate-limit gate to spawn" with NO mention of pkg/spawn/gates/ — created ratelimit.go there anyway AND extracted 173 lines from spawn_cmd.go.

**Who extracts:** Two tracks:
1. Orchestrator judgment at triage time (manual, current)
2. Daemon auto-extraction when targeting CRITICAL hotspots (automated, orch-go-TBD)

**Key insight:** Agents don't need architectural docs or rules. They need directory structure that makes the right choice obvious from search results. The file system IS the architecture.
close_reason: Design session complete. Decision: structural extraction (strangler fig) over detection/warnings. Proven by probe orch-go-8ao. Recorded as kb decision kb-c5b1dd.

title: Rework explain-back gate: conversational via orchestrator, not CLI prompts
description:
The current explain-back implementation (pkg/verify/explain_back.go) uses interactive CLI prompts - three fixed questions that Dylan types answers to in a terminal. This doesnt match how Dylan actually works: he works through the AI orchestrator conversationally.

CURRENT (wrong model):
  Dylan runs orch complete → CLI prompts 'What did you build?' → Dylan types answer → repeat x3

CORRECT (matches actual workflow):
  Orchestrator presents 'the what' → Dylan explains 'the so-what' conversationally → orchestrator captures explanation → orchestrator runs orch complete --explain 'summary'

CHANGES NEEDED:
1. Remove interactive PromptExplainBack() stdin/stdout prompting from pkg/verify/explain_back.go
2. Change --skip-explain-back flag to --explain 'text' flag on orch complete
3. Gate on --explain being non-empty when explain-back gate is active
4. Store the explanation as beads comment (this part already works)
5. Keep GateExplainBack constant and skip logic for orchestrator sessions / untracked agents

The conversational quality check (is Dylans explanation sufficient?) stays with the AI orchestrator, not the CLI. The CLIs job is: accept explanation, store it, gate on non-empty.

WHY: Fixed CLI questions can be answered mechanically (rubber-stamping). Conversational verification cant - the orchestrator can follow up, challenge, ask 'what worries you about it?' This was validated in the design session where we verified 4 deliverables conversationally and it worked well.

CONSTRAINT: AI sycophancy undermines explain-back verification (kb-afa611). The orchestrator must not mirror Dylans answers back or validate incomplete understanding.

Prior commit: 61ee67c3 (feat: add explain-back verification gate to orch complete)
Files to modify: pkg/verify/explain_back.go, cmd/orch/complete_cmd.go

title: Sibling agents in tmux die when orch complete runs for another agent
description: Problem: When orch complete runs for agent A, sibling agents B and C (running in the same workers-orch-go tmux session) die. Their tmux windows disappear and they show as phantom in orch status. This has happened repeatedly this session — every time we complete an agent, other in-flight agents die. The t95 fix (overmind restart api instead of kill) landed but agents still die. Evidence from this session: (1) orch-go-9 was Phase: Implementing when orch-go-8 completion killed it. (2) orch-go-7 was Phase: Implementing when orch-go-8 completion killed it. (3) Earlier: pw-8966 and orch-go-1 died the same way. Timeline: agents spawned ~23:30, orch-go-8 completed at ~23:56, orch-go-9 and orch-go-7 went phantom immediately after. Possible causes: (1) make install replaces the orch binary mid-execution for active agents. (2) overmind restart api has side effects on tmux. (3) Claude CLI sessions crash when OpenCode server restarts. (4) Some other orch complete side effect kills sibling windows.

title: Headless spawn doesn't set agent working directory to --workdir/cwd
description: Problem: When spawning headless agents for cross-project work (e.g., from orch-go orchestrator targeting price-watch), the agent's shell starts in orch-go (likely the daemon's cwd or a hardcoded default) instead of the target project directory. PROJECT_DIR is included in SPAWN_CONTEXT.md as prompt text, but this doesn't control the actual working directory of the spawned session. The agent has to manually cd, and bd commands fail before it does (e.g., 'bd comment pw-w7z0' fails because it's looking in orch-go's .beads/ which has no pw- prefix issues). Evidence: Agent pw-feat-build-scsoauthclient-service-13feb-7c9d started in /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go instead of /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/work/SendCutSend/scs-special-projects/price-watch. SPAWN_CONTEXT.md had correct PROJECT_DIR but shell cwd was wrong. Agent had to cd manually and bd comment failed before it did. Impact: P1 because this affects ALL cross-project headless spawns. Agents waste context discovering wrong directory, bd phase reporting fails, and early commands operate on wrong project's beads DB. Fix: The headless spawn backend (OpenCode HTTP API) needs to set the session's working directory to the --workdir flag value or the orchestrator's cwd at spawn time.

title: Fix orch clean gap: call IsSessionProcessing for ALL untracked sessions
description: cleanUntrackedDiskSessions() in clean_cmd.go:464-485 has a 5-minute recency threshold. Sessions idle >5min WITHOUT a .session_id workspace file bypass the IsSessionProcessing() check entirely and get DELETED. Interactive/TUI orchestrator sessions have no workspace, so they have zero Layer 1 protection. Fix: call IsSessionProcessing() for ALL untracked sessions, not just recently active ones. Cost: one API call per untracked session. See model: .kb/models/session-deletion-vectors.md (Vector #2)
close_reason: Fixed Vector #2 gap by removing 5-min recency threshold. Tests: go test ./cmd/orch/... - 152 passed, 0 failed, 1 skipped (0.44s). Commit: b6a48213.
